title: Default Parameters and Keyword Arguments
subtitle: Make function calls more flexible with defaults and named arguments
learning_objectives: Define a function with a default parameter value
Call a function omitting an argument to use its default
Override a default value by providing an argument
Use keyword arguments to pass values by name in any order
Explain why default parameters must come after non-default parameters

sections.heading: Default Parameter Values
sections.content:
You can give a parameter a default value by writing parameter=value in the function definition. If the caller does not supply that argument, Python uses the default. If the caller does supply a value, the supplied value overrides the default.

A key rule: parameters with defaults must appear after all parameters without defaults.
sections.code_example:
def greet(name, greeting="Hello"):
    print(greeting + ", " + name + "!")

greet("Alice")            # uses default greeting
greet("Alice", "Hi")     # overrides default
greet("Bob", "Hey")      # another override
sections.code_output:
Hello, Alice!
Hi, Alice!
Hey, Bob!
sections.warning: Never use a mutable value (like a list or dict) as a default parameter — Python creates it once and reuses the same object across all calls. Use None as the default and create the list inside the function instead.

sections.heading: Keyword Arguments
sections.content: Normally arguments are matched to parameters by position. With keyword arguments, you use the parameter name explicitly when calling the function: func(param=value). This lets you pass arguments in any order and makes calls easier to read.
sections.code_example:
def describe(name, age, city):
    print(name + " is " + str(age) + " from " + city)

# Positional call — order matters
describe("Alice", 30, "Paris")

# Keyword call — order doesn't matter
describe(city="Tokyo", name="Bob", age=25)
sections.code_output:
Alice is 30 from Paris
Bob is 25 from Tokyo

exercises.title: Power Function with Default Exponent
exercises.prompt:
Define a function called power that takes a parameter base and a parameter exp with a default value of 2. It should return base ** exp.
Call it as power(3) (uses default) and power(2, 10) (overrides default). Print both results.
exercises.starter_code: # Define power(base, exp=2) and call it twice
exercises.solution:
def power(base, exp=2):
    return base ** exp

print(power(3))
print(power(2, 10))
hints: def power(base, exp=2): — exp has a default of 2
power(3) should print 9; power(2, 10) should print 1024

exercises.title: Order a Drink
exercises.prompt:
Define a function called order_drink with parameters drink and size (default "medium"). It should print: Ordering a [size] [drink].
Call it once using a keyword argument for size and once using the default.
exercises.starter_code: # Define order_drink(drink, size="medium") and call it twice
exercises.solution:
def order_drink(drink, size="medium"):
    print("Ordering a " + size + " " + drink + ".")

order_drink("coffee", size="large")
order_drink("tea")
hints: Use a keyword argument for the first call: order_drink("coffee", size="large")
Second call omits size so the default is used: order_drink("tea")

quiz.question: Which function definition is valid Python?
quiz.explanation: Parameters with default values must come after parameters without defaults. Option A is correct. Option B would cause a SyntaxError because a non-default parameter (port) follows a default parameter (host=80).

quiz.question:
What is printed by: def show(a, b=5): print(a + b)
show(b=3, a=10)
quiz.explanation: Keyword arguments let you pass values by name in any order. a=10 and b=3, so a + b = 13.

completion_message: Fantastic! Default parameters and keyword arguments give callers the flexibility to use your functions with sensible defaults while still being able to customize behavior when needed.
